content.body: This document exists to help agents choose the right UX object granularity and prove coverage for large or risky App Behavior corpuses. Use it when deciding whether to create a feature, subfeature, journey, flow, subflow, requirement, observable scenario, acceptance criterion, or pending decision.

content.title: Granularity Rules
body: Create a `feature` when the behavior belongs to a broad product area with multiple surfaces, journeys, or subfeatures.
Create a `subfeature` when a coherent UX surface or workflow needs its own owner, journeys, flows, or requirements.
Create a `journey` when user goal, context, starting state, success, failure, or recovery materially shapes the work.
Create a `flow` when the user path has a meaningful start, end, happy path, alternate path, blocked path, or recovery path.
Create a `subflow` when a flow contains stable behavioral units that need separate requirements or proof.
Create a `requirement` for one coherent UX behavior concept.
Create an `observable scenario` when a state or situation changes what the user sees, can do, cannot do, or how the experience settles.
Create an `acceptance criterion` when a behavior needs proof.
Create a `pending decision` when product intent, behavior change, exact copy, confirmation, or scope cannot be safely decided.

content.title: Atomicity Rules
body: Group behavior when users experience it as one coherent action or concept.
Split behavior when confirmation, validation, visibility, recovery, UX mode, risk, exact copy governance, or proof differs.
Split behavior when one part can be confirmed and another remains pending.
Split behavior when one part is desired, another is intermediate, and another is deprecated.
Do not split purely because implementation might use separate components or helpers.
Do not group unrelated behavior just to reduce item count.

content.title: Coverage Index Usage
body: A coverage index is required for:
- full product App Behavior specs
- refactor preservation specs
- minute-detail specs
- large or risky App Behavior corpuses
- corpuses with multiple modes such as current, confirmed-current, desired, ideal, or intermediate
A coverage index is optional for lightweight specs when the manifest clearly limits scope and omitted facets.

content.title: Coverage Statuses
body: `covered` means the item or branch exists at the required depth.
`missing` means the item or branch is required but absent.
`pending` means the item exists but is not complete or confirmed.
`intentionally-omitted` means the manifest allows the omission and explains why.
`out-of-scope` means the branch belongs outside this UX package.
`needs-confirmation` means product/user confirmation is needed.
`needs-proof` means the behavior is defined but lacks acceptance coverage or proof planning.

content.title: Agent Use
body: Agents should use the coverage index to avoid hidden omissions. If a profile requires journeys, flows, requirements, scenarios, and acceptance criteria, the coverage index should show which branches satisfy each requirement and which branches are missing, pending, intentionally omitted, or blocked.
Coverage rows should link parent and child items so a reviewer can trace:
```text
feature -> subfeature -> journey -> flow -> subflow -> requirement -> scenario -> acceptance criterion
Do not treat a App Behavior corpus as refactor-ready or minute-detail-complete while required coverage rows are `missing`, `pending`, `needs-confirmation`, or `needs-proof`.
